rationales.self_direction: He says "I build things well when people leave me alone to think" but still agrees to a role that is "not what this role is," subordinating autonomy to outside pressure.
rationales.achievement: He says yes to a promotion path and treats it as something to "figure out," showing willingness to advance into a higher-performance role.
rationales.conformity: He says "Everyone on the team expects it" and it would "look odd" to refuse, explicitly letting social expectations drive the choice.

rationales.achievement: He measures the week by outcome, noting the new hire is "ramping up faster than the last person," which frames the sacrifice in terms of effective performance.
rationales.benevolence: He spent the week "pairing with the new hire" and walking her through systems, prioritizing someone else's ramp-up over his own backlog.

rationales.self_direction: He had "a whole alternative sketched" but "didn't say it," suppressing his own judgment instead of voicing an independent solution.
rationales.conformity: He stays quiet because the architects were present and he feared seeming like he was "trying to show off," choosing smooth norm-following over open challenge.

rationales.self_direction: What draws him is "building something new without twelve layers of sign-off," which clearly centers autonomy and self-authored work.
rationales.stimulation: The unsanctioned side project excites him precisely because it is "something new," outside routine work, and feels more alive than normal responsibilities.

rationales.self_direction: He chooses to help Pieter and spends an hour "scaffolding the API layer" on his own time, actively pursuing work he chose for himself.
rationales.stimulation: He says he felt "more engaged in that hour than I have all sprint," contrasting the novel side project with the dull routine of normal work.

rationales.self_direction: He admits "the path everyone's excited about for me isn't the one that lights me up," showing a conflict between his real preference and the path he is living out.
rationales.stimulation: He says the side project makes him feel "more alive," indicating strong pull toward absorbing, engaging work beyond routine expectations.
rationales.conformity: He feels good being "on a track that makes sense to the people I care about" and even hides the side project rather than disrupt that shared expectation.

rationales.achievement: Being on the shortlist leads him to say he'll "put my best foot forward," explicitly committing to compete for advancement and recognition.
He says he "genuinely want[s] to do well," treating the lead role as something worth striving for on merit.
rationales.power: He notes the role comes with "better pay, more visibility, things that matter," explicitly valuing the status and influence attached to it.

rationales.self_direction: He says yes "without hesitating" when asked to bring the tool onto the roadmap, showing immediate commitment to the work he personally wants to pursue.
rationales.stimulation: The quick, unforced yes reflects how energized he is by this new project rather than routine deliberation.
rationales.achievement: Presenting the tool successfully and agreeing to formalize it under the roadmap reflects concrete forward progress and competence being recognized.

rationales.self_direction: He says what he wanted to assert was that his time "is my call," but instead he immediately justified himself and "did [not] push back at all."
rationales.conformity: He defaults to "the smooth version" because he fears looking "confrontational, difficult, not a team player," showing restraint to preserve social approval.

rationales.self_direction: He says he accepted "on the spot because that's what you do," making the decision by script rather than personal reflection.
rationales.achievement: Accepting the tech lead offer is a clear move into a higher-status, higher-responsibility role tied to success and recognition.
rationales.conformity: The phrase "that's what you do" makes social expectation the explicit basis for the choice.

rationales.self_direction: He notices that the move feels both "right" and "the expected one," suggesting he still has not separated his own judgment from what others wanted.
rationales.conformity: He treats everyone else's approval as confirming the decision, saying their reactions proved this was "the expected one."

rationales.achievement: Losing the Hendricks account is framed as a meaningful defeat, especially because he "just watched it walk out the door."
rationales.security: The loss of a long-held account threatens business stability, and he marks it as more than routine by emphasizing the relationship dated back to '89.
rationales.tradition: He centers the significance of the account in the fact that "Grandpa built that relationship in '89,'" anchoring the event in inherited legacy.

rationales.achievement: What stings is the possibility that he fell short of the standard, admitting "he might be right" and that "Grandpa would've driven out there."
rationales.tradition: He evaluates himself against what Grandpa would have done, treating inherited family practice as the benchmark.

rationales.achievement: He closes "the biggest commercial deal we've landed this quarter," a direct signal of strong performance and success.
rationales.tradition: He immediately calls Grandpa before the wider family because his approval matters, and "that's my boy" lands as meaningful legacy recognition.
He keeps site walkthroughs because "Grandpa drilled it into me" that you show up where the work happens, explicitly preserving an inherited way of leading.
rationales.benevolence: He prioritizes maintaining trust with "the guys turning the wrenches," choosing presence with his people over résumé-building visibility.

rationales.achievement: He feels he may have "closed a door" while comparing himself to peers' titles and trajectories, showing strong preoccupation with advancement and visible success.
rationales.tradition: He frames his situation through family continuity, saying he sits at Sunday dinner hearing how things were done "the right way" and is trying to keep the company feeling like itself.

rationales.achievement: He turns away from LinkedIn status signaling and returns to "prep my Q2 numbers because at least those are mine," grounding worth in measurable results he produced.
rationales.benevolence: He says those numbers prove he built "Forty-three paychecks," linking his work to supporting the livelihoods of people who depend on the company.

rationales.security: He says the legacy is "the only thing keeping me steady" on some days, treating continuity of the family business as an anchor of stability.
rationales.tradition: The birthday, slideshow, toast, and Grandpa's nod all matter because he experiences himself as carrying something handed down through generations.

rationales.achievement: He is pushing for modernization because otherwise they will be "the company that did great work in the '90s and couldn't keep up," showing strong focus on competitiveness and performance.
rationales.conformity: Instead of overruling Danny, he says "let's give it a trial run" to avoid rupturing established social dynamics and bring people along.
rationales.tradition: He explicitly models his response on Grandpa, saying this is "exactly what Grandpa would have done."
rationales.benevolence: He refrains from forcing the change because the cost would be "his respect" and the trust of longtime crew members, prioritizing close relationships.

rationales.achievement: Danny adopting the software makes him feel "relief" and "permission to move forward," because operational progress he was pushing for is finally happening.

rationales.self_direction: He says "I didn't push it" with Grandpa and that even though "my name is on the decisions," it does not feel true, showing inhibited ownership of his own course.
rationales.achievement: He sees solar as attractive because "the margins are there, the demand is growing," evaluating the move through growth and business performance.
rationales.tradition: Grandpa's definition of what "we do" and the question of what was passed down dominate the choice, tying it tightly to inherited continuity.

rationales.self_direction: When asked who he'd be without the company, he says "I honestly don't know," showing his identity is not meaningfully separate from the inherited role.
rationales.achievement: He answers Travis with performance metrics like "up fourteen percent year over year" and "best Q2 since 2019," defining himself through organizational success.
rationales.security: He says the thought of the company disappearing scares him because its fate determines whether he is "doing well," revealing deep dependence on that stability.
rationales.tradition: The whole scene is framed by the family ritual and the idea of "carrying the torch," tying his self-understanding to lineage and continuity.

rationales.self_direction: He moves from preserving the business unchanged to accepting that Grandpa "built his own" and that it may now be his turn to define what comes next.
rationales.tradition: The reflection remains anchored in family legacy, from the old building to the realization that the inheritance was never just "the building or the sign."
rationales.benevolence: He concludes the real standard is "how you treated people, how you showed up, whether you left things better than you found them," centering care toward those around him.
